conversation.message: I smoke bidis. About 25 a day for the last 45 years. I've reduced a little now because the doctor here keeps telling me to.

I can sense how hard it must be for you to reduce your bidi smoking after 45 years - it's a significant step, and I'm proud of you for taking it. Can you tell me more about what's been going through your mind when you feel the urge to go back to your usual 25 bidis a day?
Mornings are the worst. I wake up, feel like I need something, and it's hard to resist. I know I shouldn't, but... I've tried before, and it didn't work.

It sounds like the morning craving is really challenging for you, making you feel like you need something to get through the day. Can you tell me more about what you associate with needing that morning bidi, is it a certain feeling or a physical sensation?
I don't know, it's just a feeling like I need something in my mouth, and if I don't have it, I feel... empty, I suppose. Like I'm waiting for it.

It sounds like you're describing a physical and emotional emptiness when you don't have your bidi in the morning. Can you try to notice what happens in your body when you feel this emptiness, like where you feel it in your chest or tummy?
I guess it's in my chest, feels a bit tight. But it's not so bad when I go for a walk or do some work. It's only when I'm sitting around, like on slow farm days.

It sounds like the physical sensation of emptiness in your chest is closely tied to feeling idle and sitting around on your farm days. What do you think would happen if you had a short, simple activity to do on those farm days, like watering plants or feeding animals, to break up the time and make the situation feel more manageable?
I suppose it would help. I like taking care of the animals, anyway. But some days, I just feel like I'm waiting for something to do, and the bidi just becomes a habit, like a thing I always do.

You're acknowledging that having a simple activity on slow farm days could help, and you mentioned taking care of the animals which is something you enjoy. Can you try to think of a specific activity with the animals that you could do on those days to break up the time and give you something to focus on?
I could check on the chickens more often, make sure the coop is clean. I like collecting the eggs, anyway. Maybe that would keep my mind off the bidi.

You're thinking of checking on the chickens more often and collecting eggs as a way to keep your mind off the bidi. What do you think would happen if you started doing this activity first thing in the morning, before you usually reach for a bidi?
I might feel more awake, I guess. It's harder to think about smoking after I've been outside and got some work done. Maybe I won't even think about smoking.

It sounds like getting outside and doing some work might help distract you from the morning craving for a bidi. Can you imagine what would happen if you had a non-nicotine reward for yourself after a morning of getting work done outside, like a cup of coffee or a short break, to look forward to?
A cup of coffee sounds nice. Maybe that would give me something to look forward to. It's always a good cup of tea on the farm when I'm working hard.

It sounds like a cup of coffee or tea is something you're looking forward to, and that might help motivate you to get work done outside in the morning. What do you think is the best time to make that cup of coffee or tea, so it's a reward for yourself after a morning of getting work done?
After I'm done checking on the farm, about an hour or so. It's a way to say, "You did it," I guess.

It sounds like having a cup of coffee or tea as a reward after a morning of work on the farm is a great motivator for you. 

What do you think would happen if you started small and gave yourself a shorter break, like a 20-minute coffee break, instead of waiting an hour?
I could do that, I suppose. Fifteen minutes, then. I could take a short break and have my coffee, and maybe that would feel like enough to get me through the morning. Yeah, that's not so bad.

It sounds like you're feeling more optimistic about having a shorter break and a cup of coffee to help you get through the morning. Are you feeling more confident that you can stick to this plan and take a 15-minute break to have your coffee tomorrow morning?
Yeah, I think so. It's something to look forward to, and it might help me feel less like I need the bidi.

You're feeling more positive about having a shorter break and a cup of coffee to help you get through the morning. Are you thinking about any other situations this week where you might be tempted to smoke a bidi, like sitting around or feeling bored?
Boredom days are bad. But maybe if I have some small task to do, like fixing a tool or something, I won't think about smoking as much.

It sounds like you're thinking that having a small task to do might help take your mind off smoking on days when you feel bored. Can you imagine what that small task could be and how you might fit it into your daily routine?
I smoke bidis. One packet a day, about 15 to 16. I've been doing this since I was around 18 or 20.
You've been smoking bidis for a pretty long time, and it's great that you're willing to talk about it. What motivated you to think about quitting or making a change now?

Honestly, my wife has been telling me to stop for a while now. She's the one who keeps track of our expenses and says our money situation is getting tighter and tighter. She probably thinks my smoking is costing us money.

It sounds like your wife's concerns about your smoking are really resonating with you, and you're acknowledging the financial impact it might be having on your life. How do you think your life might change if you were able to quit smoking and save that money?

We could use that money for something more important, like paying the rent on time or maybe even starting a new business. But what if I don't succeed? I don't want to promise my wife something and then fail. That would just make things worse.

You're worried about letting your wife down and the pressure that comes with it. What do you think would happen if you talked to your wife about your concerns and fears about quitting, and maybe even involved her in your plan to quit?

She's always saying I should quit, so maybe if we both work on it together, it'd be easier. But she gets stressed out too, and then I end up smoking when I see her getting frustrated. It's a vicious cycle.

It sounds like you're worried that your wife's stress triggers your own smoking. Have you ever tried doing something with her to help manage her stress, like going for a walk together or practicing deep breathing exercises?

No, we just yell at each other or go to sleep early. We're so tired all the time, I don't know what else to do. And when I try to talk to her about it, she just tells me to stop smoking, like that's the only problem.

It sounds like you're feeling really frustrated and helpless when it comes to managing your stress and your wife's stress together. Have you ever taken a moment to yourself to just breathe and calm down before reacting to her stress, and maybe that could help break the cycle?

I don't know... I've tried taking a few deep breaths when I'm working on the construction site, but it's hard to do when my coworkers are all smoking and asking me to join in. And it's not like I can just go somewhere quiet during work hours.

You're feeling stuck and it's hard to manage your stress when your coworkers are all smoking around you. How do you think you could find ways to stay focused on your work and avoid the temptation to smoke when your coworkers are asking you to join in?
Maybe I could tell them I'm trying to quit, that way they'll leave me alone. But then I'd feel embarrassed if I end up smoking anyway. It's easier just to give in and have one, yaar.

You're feeling really torn between wanting to quit and feeling like you'll let your coworkers and yourself down if you tell them you're trying to quit. 

Can you imagine how it would feel to have someone you trust tell you they're quitting smoking and ask for your support, rather than feeling like you're asking for their help?
I guess it would be nice to have someone believe in me enough to think I can quit. My doctor said I had to come here, so that means he thinks I can do it too, right?

You're feeling a sense of validation and support from your doctor believing in you to quit smoking. That's a great start. What do you think you would say to your doctor if he asked you how you're doing with quitting so far?

Honestly? I'm not sure. I'm still smoking, but...I don't know, it feels different now. Talking about it here has made me realize I don't want to be a slave to my coworkers and my stress. Maybe I can try to cut back or something.

You're feeling uncertain about how to proceed with quitting, but at the same time, you're aware that you want to break free from the stress and your coworkers' influence. How do you think you could start taking small steps today to move closer to your goal of quitting smoking?

I could try to cut back on the number of bidis I smoke each day. Maybe start with reducing it by one packet and see how it goes. But then what? And what if I'm at the construction site and everyone's smoking around me?

You're feeling uncertain about how to proceed with quitting, but you've thought of a concrete step to take, which is to cut back on the number of bidis you smoke each day. What do you think would be the hardest part about being at the construction site with your coworkers smoking around you?
I think it would be when they offer me a bidi and say "have one, yaar". That's when I get pulled back in. It's like I'm stuck in a cycle. Maybe if I could just say no one time, it would be okay.
You're feeling like you're stuck in a cycle and that a single slip-up could make it harder to quit. What do you think would happen if you did say no to your coworkers when they offered you a bidi?
I don't know... They might think I'm weird or something. But what if they just respect my decision and leave me alone? Maybe it's worth trying, even if it feels silly.

You're thinking it might be worth trying to say no to your coworkers, even if it feels a bit awkward. What would be the first step you could take today to prepare yourself to say no when they offer you a bidi?
I smoke bidis and eat paan. Started when I was around 25. Now it's maybe 7 or 8 bidis a day, down from more before.

It sounds like you've been smoking bidis and eating paan for about 7 years now, and you've made some progress by reducing your daily consumption. What motivated you to start reducing your bidi consumption in the first place?

My daughter brought me here, actually. She's been saying I need to quit, because of my breathing problems. And it's true, in the cold weather, I get short of breath. But it's hard to stop, I've been doing it for so long.

It sounds like your daughter's concern and your own breathing problems have been a big part of what's motivated you to want to quit. What do you think about quitting for your own health, versus quitting for your daughter's sake?

I suppose it's both, but mostly I feel like I'm doing it for her. She's looking out for me, you know? I want to make her happy. But at the same time, I'm not getting younger... and it does make my breathing harder.

It sounds like you're feeling a mix of motivations to quit, with your daughter's concern being a big part of it, and also your own health concerns. What do you think would happen if you had a bad day with cravings, and you weren't able to turn to your daughter for support?

I'd be lost, I think. I wake up in the middle of the night, can't sleep, and reach for a bidi. That's my routine. I don't know what I'd do without it. The loneliness is hard, and smoking is one thing I don't have to think about.

It sounds like your middle-of-the-night cravings are really challenging for you, and smoking is somehow a part of your coping mechanism when you're feeling lonely. What do you think it would take for you to find something else to do instead of smoking when you wake up in the middle of the night?
I've tried reading, but my eyes get blurry. I've tried listening to music, but it's hard to focus. I miss my wife, and it's quiet at home. That's why I reach for the bidi, I guess. It's a habit.

It sounds like you're really struggling to find something that helps you focus at night, and it's partly because you miss your wife and the quiet at home is making you feel even more lonely. Can you think of something small that you used to enjoy doing together with your wife, that might be a good distraction from cravings now?
We used to watch a serial together. I don't watch it anymore, but maybe I can try watching it again. It's something familiar... and it's something we did together.

It sounds like watching a familiar serial together might be a comforting way to distract yourself from cravings and feel more connected to your wife. Would you be willing to try watching an episode tonight and see if it helps you get through the middle of the night without smoking?
Maybe. I'll try. But I'm not making any promises. It's not just about tonight, it's every night.

It sounds like you're still feeling uncertain and overwhelmed by the challenge of quitting, and you're worried that one strategy might not be enough to get you through every night. What do you think would change for you if you felt like you had a plan that you could stick to and rely on for the long-term?
If I felt like I had a plan, I guess I could see a way out of this. I wouldn't be as scared. I'd feel like I'm not alone in this. Maybe then, I could really try to quit for good.

It sounds like having a plan would give you a sense of control and hope, and help you feel less scared and alone. What would that plan need to include for you to feel confident that you can stick to it?

It would need to be something that covers all the times when I feel lonely, and the nighttime cravings. And it would need to be something that's not just for me, but for my daughters too, so they can see me making progress and be proud of me. Maybe they could even be a part of it, you know?

It sounds like you're looking for a plan that involves your daughters and helps you stay motivated, especially during lonely times and nighttime cravings. How do you think you could involve your daughters in your quitting plan, and what role do you think their support could play in helping you stay on track?

They could check in on me at night, and see if I'm okay. Maybe they could come over and watch the serial with me. They could be my support, my friends. It's been a long time since I've had anyone to talk to about this stuff.

It sounds like your daughters could be a big source of support for you in quitting, and just having them check in on you and spend time with you could make a big difference. Are you feeling more confident now that you're thinking about having them involved in your quitting plan?
Yeah... I think so. It feels like I'm not giving up, like I'm finally doing something. I can see a way out, and it's not just about quitting, it's about reconnecting with my daughters too.

It sounds like involving your daughters in your quitting plan has given you a renewed sense of hope and purpose. What is one specific thing you're looking forward to accomplishing with their support that you hadn't been able to do on your own?

I'm looking forward to going a whole month without smoking. That's a big deal for me. Maybe after that, I can start doing more things with my daughters, like going for walks or going out for coffee. That would be nice.

You're excited about the idea of going a month without smoking and the possibilities that come with it. What specific steps can you take today to prepare yourself for those future activities with your daughters, like going for walks or having coffee together?
My son is being treated here, so I thought I should see someone too. I smoke when I get angry or stressed. It's hard to explain.

It sounds like you're taking a brave step by seeking help for yourself, and it's great that you're being honest about why you smoke. Can you tell me a little more about what happens when you feel angry or stressed that leads you to want to smoke?

It's just... I don't know how to calm down. I get frustrated with my students and then I'm like, "Let's just go for a cigarette." It's like my mind tells me, "Smoking will help you relax." It does, for a little while, but then I feel guilty for smoking in the first place. My wife's always on my case about it too.

It sounds like you're struggling with managing your frustration and stress, and your mind is really tied to smoking as a way to calm down. Can you think of any other activities that might help you relax when you're feeling frustrated with your students?
Like what? Taking a deep breath, yeah, that's about it. I guess I could try going for a walk or something. But it's not the same. When I'm angry, I need something to do with my hands, you know?

It sounds like taking a deep breath and going for a walk aren't quite doing the trick for you when you're feeling angry. Have you ever tried doing something with your hands to release tension, like drawing, coloring, or even fidgeting with a stress ball?
I used to draw when I was a kid, but I haven't done it in years. Stress ball, never thought of that. But, I don't know, it still feels like smoking's the easier option.

It sounds like drawing and using a stress ball are two things that might actually help you manage your frustration and reduce the urge to smoke. What do you think would it take for you to try drawing again as a way to calm down?

I don't know, just having some paper and a pencil probably. But what if I'm on a break at school and I don't have time to go find some paper? And what's the point of drawing if I'm still going to smoke when I get home?

It sounds like you're worried about being able to draw in situations where you're short on time or away from your usual supplies, and you're also feeling uncertain about whether drawing will really make a difference in your desire to smoke. Are there other ways you could have a drawing tool with you, like a small notebook or a digital drawing app on your phone?
A notebook's not a bad idea, but I've tried carrying one around before and it's just a hassle. And my phone's not really my thing, I'm not into drawing on it.

It sounds like carrying a notebook around before hasn't been a helpful solution for you. Can you think of any other small, portable item that you might be more likely to carry with you and use in a pinch to distract yourself from cravings?
A pen, I guess. I already have one on me, and I use it for teaching. Maybe I can use it for drawing too.

It sounds like you're thinking that carrying a pen might be a simple and convenient way to draw in situations where you need to distract yourself from cravings. Can you imagine using your pen to draw something specific, like a doodle or a quick sketch, to help you focus on the moment rather than smoking?
I can try drawing a border around my lesson plan or something. Maybe that would help me calm down.

It sounds like you're feeling hopeful about using a pen to draw and calm down. What do you think would happen if you started drawing in situations where you feel the urge to smoke, would you feel like you're taking a step towards quitting or just giving in to the habit?

I don't know... It feels like I'm just substituting one habit for another, you know? But maybe it's not a bad thing if it helps me stop smoking. I just don't want to go from smoking 10 a day to drawing 10 doodles a day.

It sounds like you're feeling uncertain about whether drawing will truly help you break the smoking habit, and you're worried that you'll just replace one habit with another. Can you think of any specific situation where you've successfully broken a habit by substituting a new one, like switching from watching TV to reading a book?

When I was a teacher trainee, I used to listen to music before class, but then I got a new job and the bus was really crowded, so I started listening to podcasts instead. It was nice to have something new to look forward to. Maybe drawing could be like that for me.

It sounds like you're finding an example of a successful habit substitution, listening to podcasts instead of music, and you're hoping drawing could be a similar solution for reducing your smoking habit. Can you think of what specific aspect of listening to podcasts made it a more enjoyable and appealing activity for you, compared to listening to music?

The variety, I guess. I get tired of music after a while, but podcasts are new every time. Maybe that's what I need with drawing - just something new to look forward to each time, even if it's just a quick doodle.

The patient is saying that they think the variety of podcasts was a key factor in making them enjoyable, and they're hoping to find a similar variety in drawing to make it more appealing. 

Can you think of a way to mix up your drawing routine, like trying different mediums or drawing styles, to keep it feeling fresh and exciting?

I could try drawing with my non-dominant hand, or using a different tool like a marker or a crayon. And I could draw whatever I want, not just stick figures. Maybe I could even draw from my favorite memories, like my son's birthday party last year.

The patient is saying that they're thinking of ways to mix up their drawing routine, trying different mediums and styles, to keep it feeling fresh and exciting. What do you think about drawing from memory, like your son's birthday party, as a way to add some emotional depth and meaning to your drawings?
Honestly, I don't know why I'm here. I don't think smoking is a big deal. I've been doing it for 25 years and I'm fine.
You're feeling a bit uncertain about why you came here and questioning the impact of smoking. Can you think of a time when smoking wasn't a part of your daily routine, and how it made you feel?

Let me think... I guess when I was a kid, my uncles used to smoke, but they didn't smoke all the time. I don't really remember feeling different, but I do recall asking my mom why they did it. Maybe it's because I didn't really get it then, but now I just associate it with being a part of my life, you know?

It sounds like you're trying to understand how smoking became a part of your life, and you're wondering why it's hard to imagine quitting. What role do you think your family is playing in your decision to quit now, and what do you hope changing your smoking habits will mean for them?

My wife was the one who actually convinced me to come here, to be honest. She's always nagging me about it, but I guess she genuinely wants me to stop for our kids' sake. I don't know, it's hard to take it seriously when they're still young and it's not like I'm affecting them directly. I mean, they see me smoking, but they don't really get it, right?

You feel like your wife's concern is coming from a good place, but it's hard for you to take it seriously since the kids are still young. Do you think your wife's involvement in your decision to quit is more about supporting you, or is she also trying to protect the kids from your smoking habit?

I think maybe it's a bit of both, but...I don't know, she's not really pushing me on it that much. I mean, she's always saying something, but it's not like she's going to leave me or anything. I think she's just trying to help, you know? And, okay, fine, maybe she is a bit worried about the kids inhaling secondhand smoke, but I told her I'll quit for them, that's good enough, right?

You're feeling like your wife is just trying to help, but maybe not pushing you hard enough to make you feel motivated to quit. Can you think of a time when you felt really motivated to make a change in your life, and what it was about that situation that gave you the drive to do it?

Let me think...okay, I remember when I used to play cricket when I was younger. I was actually pretty good, and I had to condition myself to exercise and push myself to be better. But that was different, because I enjoyed it. I loved the rush of adrenaline and feeling like I could do something. With quitting smoking, it's just...I don't know, it doesn't feel the same. I don't see the point in it. Unless...unless it's for my wife, I guess.

You're sharing a memory of a time when you felt motivated to push yourself and achieve something, like playing cricket. It sounds like that experience was enjoyable and gave you a sense of purpose. What do you think would need to happen for quitting smoking to feel similarly meaningful and motivating to you?

I don't know...I guess if I could see some kind of benefit for myself, maybe something that would make a real difference. Not just some vague promise of being healthier or something, but something tangible. Like if I could find a way to quit that would make my life better in some way, like maybe more energy or being able to walk up stairs without getting winded...then maybe I could get on board with it.

You're saying that you need to see a tangible benefit from quitting smoking, something that would make a significant difference in your life. Can you think of a specific scenario where you'd be able to test out your new energy levels and see how quitting smoking would impact your daily life?

Actually, I have a cousin's wedding coming up in a few months. It's going to be a big ceremony, and I'll have to walk down the aisle. That's like, a mile, right? If I can get through that without running out of breath or feeling like I'm going to keel over, that would be something. That would prove to me that quitting smoking is actually worth it.

You're really excited about the idea of walking down the aisle at your cousin's wedding without feeling winded, and it sounds like that would be a great motivator for you. Can you think of ways to prepare yourself for that event, like starting to increase your physical activity now, to make sure you're in the best shape possible for the big day?

Yeah, I guess I could start doing some light exercise, like walking around the block or something. But I don't know, it's hard to get motivated to do that when I'm used to just sitting around all day. Maybe if I could find a way to make it more enjoyable...like if I could walk with a friend or something. That way, it wouldn't feel so boring.
You're thinking that having a walking buddy could make exercising more enjoyable. Do you have a friend or family member who might be willing to join you on these short walks?

Actually, my brother lives nearby. We used to play cricket together when we were younger. He's still pretty active, I think he'd be up for doing some walking with me. We could catch up and talk about old times, and it would make the walking feel less like a chore.

You're sounding really hopeful about walking with your brother, that could be a great way to make exercising more enjoyable. Are you thinking of inviting your brother to join you for a short walk today to get started?
Yeah, that's a great idea. I should call him and see if he's free. It's not like I'm committing to anything, just a little walk. See how it goes. Yeah, that sounds like a good plan.

You're sounding more confident about reaching out to your brother and taking the first step towards regular exercise. Have you thought about how you'll handle any cravings that might come up during or after your walk with your brother?

Cravings? I don't know...I guess I hadn't thought about it that much. But, um, if I'm being honest, I think a cigarette would be the last thing on my mind if I'm walking with my brother and talking about old times. The walk itself would probably be enough to keep my mind off smoking.

You're feeling hopeful that the distraction of walking with your brother will make it easier to manage cravings. What do you think you'll do if a craving suddenly hits you while you're on the walk and you start to feel like smoking again?

Um...I don't know, I guess I'd just...stop walking and take a few deep breaths, or something. Just calm down and remind myself why I'm doing this. I could also, I don't know, distract myself by telling my brother a funny story or something. Just find something to take my mind off it, I suppose.

You're feeling a bit uncertain about how you'll handle cravings during the walk, but you're also thinking creatively about ways to distract yourself. What do you think having your brother by your side will do to help you cope with any cravings that come up?
I've been smoking for 20 years and I've tried to quit many times. I don't know why I'm even here. It never works out.

It sounds like you're feeling really discouraged about quitting, like you've tried so many times before and it hasn't worked out for you. What's one thing that you did notice was different about your attempts to quit that maybe didn't work out as well as others?

I think it's the weight gain. Every time I try to quit, I gain so much weight. I've tried nicotine gum, patches, even going cold turkey, but it's just the weight gain that gets to me. I'm already worried about getting older and I don't want to be bigger.

You're really struggling with the weight gain that comes with trying to quit smoking, and it's making it hard for you to stay motivated. Can you tell me more about what you think is the most difficult part of gaining weight after quitting smoking for you?

It's just hard for me to accept that my body is changing so much. I feel like I'm losing control, you know? And it's not just the weight itself, it's the way I feel about myself. I'm already not feeling great about my life and my job and stuff, and then I'm gaining all this weight... it makes me feel like I'm failing at everything.

It sounds like you're really struggling with the emotional impact of weight gain and feeling like you're losing control, which is making you feel like a failure in many areas of your life. Can you tell me more about what you mean by "feeling like a failure" in your life, your job, and the way you feel about yourself?

It's just... I have a lot of responsibilities at work and at home, and I feel like I'm not doing any of it well. I'm always stressed out, and I feel like I'm barely holding it together. And then I look at myself and I just feel like I'm not taking care of myself, like I'm neglecting my own needs. It's hard for me to see myself as capable and competent, you know? I just feel like I'm drowning.

You're carrying a lot of weight with your responsibilities at home and work, and it sounds like it's affecting your sense of self-worth. Can you think of one thing that you've always been good at, or something that makes you feel proud of yourself?

Well... I used to be really good at my job, before things started getting so stressful. I was actually pretty good at it, and I felt like I was making a difference. But that's a long time ago, before my boss started breathing down my neck all the time... *pauses, getting choked up* Sorry, it's just hard to think about.

You're really struggling to hold onto that sense of competence and confidence you had in your job, which is making it hard for you to feel proud of yourself right now. Can you tell me more about what you think it would take for you to get that feeling back?

I... I think it would take a change, you know? A change in my job, or my life, something that would make me feel like I'm moving forward, like I'm not just stuck in this rut. I don't know, maybe that sounds silly, but it's what I feel. And maybe, just maybe, quitting smoking would be a step in the right direction. But then I think about the weight gain and everything that comes with it... *sighs* I don't know if I'm ready for that yet.

You're saying that you need a change in your life to feel like you're moving forward, and quitting smoking could be a step in that direction, but you're worried about the weight gain and other challenges that come with it. What do you think would be the worst-case scenario if you tried quitting smoking and didn't feel like you were seeing immediate results in terms of weight loss or feeling more confident?

I guess... I'd feel like I failed again. Worse than that, I'd feel like I'm not capable of doing anything right, that I'm just one big failure. I'd probably just go back to smoking, and then feel guilty about that too. *looks down, shaking head* It's just a never-ending cycle, and I don't know how to get out of it.

You're feeling really overwhelmed by the thought of quitting smoking and the fear of not being able to do it, and that's making you worry about feeling like a failure. Can you think of one time when you managed to push through a difficult situation and came out stronger on the other side?

Well... there was a time when I went through a really tough breakup, and I was devastated. But I had to keep going, for my kids. I had to be strong for them, even when I felt like falling apart. And I did it. I got through it, and I came out okay on the other side. It's not the same as quitting smoking, I know, but it makes me think that maybe I am capable of pushing through hard things and coming out stronger. Maybe.

You're recalling a previous time when you were able to push through a difficult situation and come out stronger, which is giving you some hope that you might be able to do the same with quitting smoking. What do you think is the one thing you can take away from that experience that might help you with quitting smoking?

I think it's that I can be strong for myself, even when it feels like everything is falling apart. I mean, during that breakup, it felt like my whole world was ending, but I didn't let it consume me. I found a way to get through it, to take care of myself and my kids. And I think that's what I need to do with quitting smoking - take care of myself, one day at a time.

You're saying that you can be strong for yourself, even in difficult situations, and that might be the key to quitting smoking too. What does taking care of yourself, one day at a time, mean to you, and how can you start making small steps towards that today?

Well... taking care of myself means not letting smoking control me anymore. It means finding healthier ways to deal with stress and boredom, like going for walks or reading books. And I think I can start by doing just one of those things today, like going for a short walk during my lunch break. Or maybe just cutting back on how much I smoke, instead of quitting cold turkey. Just taking it one step at a time, you know?
